en.extract:
The black-throated thistletail (Asthenes harterti) is a species of bird in the Furnariinae subfamily of the ovenbird family Furnariidae. It is endemic to Bolivia.


Taxonomy and systematics
The black-throated thistletail and eight other species were previously placed in genus Schizoeaca but genetic data showed that the genus is embedded within Asthenes. Some authors considered all of those taxa to be conspecific but the genetic data confirm they are separate species.
According to the International Ornithological Committee, the black-throated thistletail has two subspecies, the nominate A. h. harterti (Berlepsch, 1901) and A. h. bejaranoi (Remsen, 1981). The Clements taxonomy treats it as monotypic. This article follows the two-subspecies model.


Description

The black-throated thistletail is 17 to 18 cm (6.7 to 7.1 in) long and weighs 12 to 14 g (0.42 to 0.49 oz). The sexes have the same plumage. Adults of the nominate subspecies have a rufous-brown face with a light brown supercilium. Their crown, back, rump, wings, and tail are dark rufescent brown. Their tail is long and deeply forked with few barbs at the feather ends. Their chin is grayish white and their throat black with whitish streaks. Their underparts are mostly brownish gray with browner flanks and undertail coverts. Their iris is light brown to brown, their maxilla blackish to dark gray, their mandible gray to pinkish gray with a dark tip, and their legs and feet blue-gray to gray. Subspecies A. h. bejaranoi differs from the nominate by having some ochraceous on the face and neck, and paler underparts with ochraceous sides and flanks.


Distribution and habitat
The black-throated thistletail is found only in the Andes of western Bolivia, in the departments of La Paz, Cochabamba, and Santa Cruz. The nominate subspecies is the more northerly of the two. The species inhabits páramo grasslands, elfin forest, the ecotone at tree line, and the edge of cloudforest. In elevation it ranges between 2,900 and 3,400 m (9,500 and 11,200 ft).


Behavior


Movement
The black-throated thistletail is a year-round resident throughout its range.


Feeding
The black-throated thistletail feeds mostly on arthropods, and seeds are a small part of its diet. It forages singly or in pairs, usually in the understory though sometimes higher. It takes its prey by gleaning mostly from foliage and small branches and also moss, grass, and rocks.


Breeding
The black-throated thistletail's breeding season has not been defined but includes at least January. It is thought to be monogamous. The one known nest was an oval mass with a side entrance, constructed of moss and a few twigs and placed on a clump of grass. The clutch size is thought to be two eggs. Both sexes provision nestlings but further details of the species' breeding biology are not known.


Vocalization
The black-throated thistletail's song is "a series of emphatic squeaky notes, accelerating and then slowing, descending slightly at end". Its alarm call is a "rodent-like 'pseea' ".


Status
The IUCN has assessed the black-throated thistletail as being of Least Concern. It has a restricted range and an unknown population size that is believed to be decreasing. No immediate threats have been identified. It is considered fairly common to common but having a "rather small" population. "Burning and grazing of timber-line habitats has greatly reduced the extent of appropriate habitat for this species."


References

The '''black-throated thistletail''' ('''''Asthenes harterti''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the Furnariinae subfamily of the ovenbird [[Family (taxonomy)|family]] [[Furnariidae]]. It is [[Endemism|endemic]] to [[Bolivia]].<ref name=IOC13.2>{{cite web |url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/bow/ovenbirds/ |title=Ovenbirds, woodcreepers |website=IOC World Bird List |version =v 13.2 | editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela Rasmussen |date=July 2023 |access-date=31 July 2023 }}</ref>

==Taxonomy and systematics==

The black-throated thistletail and eight other species were previously placed in genus ''Schizoeaca'' but genetic data showed that the genus is embedded within ''Asthenes''. Some authors considered all of those taxa to be [[conspecific]] but the genetic data confirm they are separate species.<ref name=BTTH-BOW>Remsen, Jr., J. V. (2020). Black-throated Thistletail (''Asthenes harterti''), version 1.0. In Birds of the World (J. del Hoyo, A. Elliott, J. Sargatal, D. A. Christie, and E. de Juana, Editors). Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY, USA. https://doi.org/10.2173/bow.bltthi1.01 retrieved 6 November 2023</ref><ref name=SACClist>Remsen, J. V., Jr., J. I. Areta, E. Bonaccorso, S. Claramunt, G. Del-Rio, A. Jaramillo, D. F. Lane, M. B. Robbins, F. G. Stiles, and K. J. Zimmer. Version 28 September 2023. A classification of the bird species of South America. American Ornithological Society. https://www.museum.lsu.edu/~Remsen/SACCBaseline.htm retrieved 20 October 2023</ref>

According to the [[International Ornithological Committee]], the black-throated thistletail has two subspecies, the [[Nominate subspecies|nominate]] ''A. h. harterti'' ([[Hans von Berlepsch|Berlepsch]], 1901) and ''A. h. bejaranoi'' ([[James Van Remsen, Jr.|Remsen]], 1981).<ref name=IOC13.2/> The [[Clements taxonomy]] treats it as [[monotypic]].<ref name=Clements2023>Clements, J. F., P.C. Rasmussen, T. S. Schulenberg, M. J. Iliff, T. A. Fredericks, J. A. Gerbracht, D. Lepage, A. Spencer, S. M. Billerman, B. L. Sullivan, and C. L. Wood. 2023. The eBird/Clements checklist of birds of the world: v2023. Downloaded from https://www.birds.cornell.edu/clementschecklist/download/ retrieved 28 October 2023</ref> This article follows the two-subspecies model.

==Description==
[[File:Asthenes harterti 587194475.jpg|thumb|Closeup picture of a black-throated thistletail.]]
The black-throated thistletail is {{convert|17|to|18|cm|in|sigfig=2|abbr=on}} long and weighs {{convert|12|to|14|g|oz|abbr=on}}. The sexes have the same plumage. Adults of the [[nominate subspecies]] have a rufous-brown face with a light brown [[supercilium]]. Their crown, back, rump, wings, and tail are dark rufescent brown. Their tail is long and deeply forked with few [[Barb (feather)|barbs]] at the feather ends. Their chin is grayish white and their throat black with whitish streaks. Their underparts are mostly brownish gray with browner flanks and undertail [[Covert feather|coverts]]. Their iris is light brown to brown, their [[maxilla]] blackish to dark gray, their [[mandible]] gray to pinkish gray with a dark tip, and their legs and feet blue-gray to gray. Subspecies ''A. h. bejaranoi'' differs from the nominate by having some ochraceous on the face and neck, and paler underparts with ochraceous sides and flanks.<ref name=BTTH-BOW/>

==Distribution and habitat==

The black-throated thistletail is found only in the Andes of western Bolivia, in the departments of [[La Paz Department (Bolivia)|La Paz]], [[Cochabamba Department|Cochabamba]], and [[Santa Cruz Department (Bolivia)|Santa Cruz]].<ref name=BTTH-BOW/> The nominate subspecies is the more northerly of the two.<ref name=IOC13.2/> The species inhabits [[páramo]] grasslands, [[elfin forest]], the [[ecotone]] at [[tree line]], and the edge of [[cloudforest]]. In elevation it ranges between {{convert|2900|and|3400|m|ft|abbr=on}}.<ref name=BTTH-BOW/>

==Behavior==
===Movement===

The black-throated thistletail is a year-round resident throughout its range.<ref name=BTTH-BOW/>

===Feeding===

The black-throated thistletail feeds mostly on [[arthropod]]s, and seeds are a small part of its diet. It forages singly or in pairs, usually in the understory though sometimes higher. It takes its prey by gleaning mostly from foliage and small branches and also moss, grass, and rocks.<ref name=BTTH-BOW/>

===Breeding===

The black-throated thistletail's breeding season has not been defined but includes at least January. It is thought to be monogamous. The one known nest was an oval mass with a side entrance, constructed of moss and a few twigs and placed on a clump of grass. The clutch size is thought to be two eggs. Both sexes provision nestlings but further details of the species' breeding biology are not known.<ref name=BTTH-BOW/>

{{birdsong|url=https://xeno-canto.org/species/Asthenes-harterti |species=black-throated thistletail}}

===Vocalization===

The black-throated thistletail's song is "a series of emphatic squeaky notes, accelerating and then slowing, descending slightly at end". Its alarm call is a "rodent-like 'pseea' ".<ref name=BTTH-BOW/>

==Status==

The [[IUCN]] has assessed the black-throated thistletail as being of Least Concern. It has a restricted range and an unknown population size that is believed to be decreasing. No immediate threats have been identified.<ref name=IUCN/> It is considered fairly common to common but having a "rather small" population. "Burning and grazing of timber-line habitats has greatly reduced the extent of appropriate habitat for this species."<ref name=BTTH-BOW/>
